## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of New York City](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City "History of New York City")

[![A pen drawing of two men in 16th-century Dutch clothing presenting an open box of items to a group of Native Americans in feather headdresses stereotypical of plains tribes.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/The_Purchase_of_Manhattan_Island.png/250px-The_Purchase_of_Manhattan_Island.png)](/wiki/File:The_Purchase_of_Manhattan_Island.png)

[Peter Minuit](/wiki/Peter_Minuit "Peter Minuit"), who purchased [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in 1626

During the [Wisconsinan glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsinan_glaciation "Wisconsinan glaciation"), the region was situated at the edge of a large [ice sheet](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") over 1,000 feet in depth. The ice sheet scraped away large amounts of soil, leaving the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") that serves as the geologic [foundation](/wiki/Foundation_(engineering) "Foundation (engineering)") for much of the New York metropolitan region today. Later on, the ice sheet would help split apart what are now Long Island and Staten Island.

[![A painting of a coastline dotted with red roof houses and a windmill, with several masted ships sailing close to shore under blue sky](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg/250px-GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)](/wiki/File:GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)

[New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), including present-day [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), in 1664, the year [England](/wiki/England "England") took control and renamed it New York

At the time of European contact the region was inhabited by [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), predominantly the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"),[[107]](#cite_note-110) and others. The Native Americans used the abundant waterways in the area for many purposes, such as fishing and trade routes. Sailing for France in 1524, [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano") was the first European to enter the local waters and encounter the residents, but he did not make landfall. [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson"), sailing for the Dutch in 1609, visited the area and built a settlement on Lower Manhattan Island that was eventually renamed [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") by [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") colonists in 1626.[[108]](#cite_note-u-s-history.com-111) In 1664, the area went under English control,[[108]](#cite_note-u-s-history.com-111)[[109]](#cite_note-112) and was later renamed *New York* after King [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") granted the lands to his brother, the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England").[[110]](#cite_note-113)[[111]](#cite_note-114)

As the fur trade expanded further north, New York became a [trading hub](/wiki/Trade "Trade"), which brought in a diverse set of ethnic groups including [Africans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_of_Africa "Ethnic groups of Africa"), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people"). The island of Manhattan had an extraordinary natural harbor formed by [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay") (actually the drowned lower river valley of the Hudson River, enclosed by [glacial moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine")), the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), which is a [tidal strait](/wiki/Tidal_strait "Tidal strait"), and the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), all of which merge at the southern tip, from which all later development spread. During the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), the strategic waterways made New York vitally important as a wartime base for the British navy. Many battles such as the [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island") and the [Battle of New York](/wiki/New_York_and_New_Jersey_campaign "New York and New Jersey campaign") were fought in the region to secure it. New York was captured by the British early in the war, becoming a haven for [Loyalist](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") refugees from other parts of the country, and remained in the hands of the British until the war ended in 1783. New York served as the [capital of the United States](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States#Former_national_capitals "List of capitals in the United States") from 1785 until 1790,[[112]](#cite_note-senate-115) after which the capital moved to Philadelphia. New York has been the country's largest city since 1790.[[113]](#cite_note-116) In 1792, the [Buttonwood Agreement](/wiki/Buttonwood_Agreement "Buttonwood Agreement"), made by a group of merchants, created what is now the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") in Lower Manhattan. Today, many people in the metropolitan area work in this important stock exchange.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Liberty-statue-from-below.jpg/250px-Liberty-statue-from-below.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberty-statue-from-below.jpg)

*Liberty Enlightening the World*, known as the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"), on [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island") in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), a globally recognized symbol of both the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and its ideals of freedom, democracy, and opportunity.[[114]](#cite_note-117)

The [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") greeted millions of [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") as they came to America by ship in the late 19th and early 20th centuries[[115]](#cite_note-118) and is a globally recognized symbol of the United States and its [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy").[[116]](#cite_note-119) Large-scale immigration into New York was a result of a large demand for manpower. A [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") attitude in the city created tolerance for various cultures and ethnic groups. German, Irish, and Italian immigrants were among the largest ethnic groups. Today, many of their descendants continue to live in the region. Cultural buildings such as the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), the [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera"), and the [American Museum of Natural History](/wiki/American_Museum_of_Natural_History "American Museum of Natural History") were built. New York newspapers were read around the country as media moguls [James Gordon Bennett, Sr.](/wiki/James_Gordon_Bennett,_Sr. "James Gordon Bennett, Sr."), [Joseph Pulitzer](/wiki/Joseph_Pulitzer "Joseph Pulitzer") and [William Randolph Hearst](/wiki/William_Randolph_Hearst "William Randolph Hearst") battled for readership. In 1884, over 70% of exports passed through ports in New York or in one of the surrounding towns. The five [boroughs](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of New York — [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") — were consolidated into a single city in 1898.[[117]](#cite_note-120)[[118]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Grand_Central_Terminal_Main_Concourse_May_2014.jpg/250px-Grand_Central_Terminal_Main_Concourse_May_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Central_Terminal_Main_Concourse_May_2014.jpg)

The main concourse of [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), which opened in 1913

The newly unified New York City encouraged both more physical connections between the boroughs and the growth of [bedroom communities](/wiki/Bedroom_community "Bedroom community"). The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") began operating in 1904 as the [Interborough Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit_Company "Interborough Rapid Transit Company"), one of three systems (the other two being the [Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn-Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit Corporation") and the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System")) that were later taken over by the city. Railroad stations such as [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") and [Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)") helped fuel suburban growth. During the era of the [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition"), when alcohol was banned nationwide, organized crime grew to supply the high demand for bootleg alcohol. The Broadway [Theater District](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan") began to develop with the opening of the [New York Subway](/wiki/New_York_Subway "New York Subway") in 1904 and, by the early part of the twentieth century, had been made world-famous as New York's theatrical and entertainment center through popular musical productions like [Ziegfeld Follies](/wiki/Ziegfeld_Follies "Ziegfeld Follies") and *[Show Boat](/wiki/Show_Boat "Show Boat")* and the opening of multiple large, extravagantly decorated theatres in the area spanning [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") from 47th to 42nd Streets.

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") suspended the region's fortunes as a period of widespread unemployment and poverty began. City planner [Robert Moses](/wiki/Robert_Moses "Robert Moses") began his automobile-centered career of building bridges, [parkways](/wiki/Parkways "Parkways"), and later [expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") across the tri-state area. During World War II, the city economy was hurt by blockades of German [U-boats](/wiki/U-boats "U-boats"), which limited shipping with Europe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/UN_Headquarters_2.jpg/250px-UN_Headquarters_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:UN_Headquarters_2.jpg)

[United Nations Headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_Headquarters "United Nations Headquarters"), established in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") in 1952

After its population peaked in 1950, a significant portion of the city's population left for the [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") of New York over the following decades. The effects were a result of [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight"). Industry and commerce also declined in this era, with businesses relocating to the suburbs or other regions. The era also saw an increase in the construction of [housing projects](/wiki/Housing_projects "Housing projects") for the city's low-income population under the [New York City Housing Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Housing_Authority "New York City Housing Authority"), coinciding with the destruction of communities to construct [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_highways "Interstate highways") to link the city with its suburbs. The city, particularly Brooklyn, was dealt a psychological as well as an economic blow with the loss of the iconic [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") major-league [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") team, which moved to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") after the 1957 season. Crime affected the city severely. Urban renewal projects alleviated decay in poorer neighborhoods to a certain extent, but many of these later proved to be failures and caused unanticipated consequences like ghettoization, informal racial segregation in housing, and disruption of the organic urban fabric that made the city's neighborhoods cohesive and healthy places to live. There was little reported social unrest during the [Northeast Blackout of 1965](/wiki/Northeast_Blackout_of_1965 "Northeast Blackout of 1965"), but the [New York City Blackout of 1977](/wiki/New_York_City_Blackout_of_1977 "New York City Blackout of 1977") led to massive rioting, looting, and arson in some parts of the city. In addition, the [1970s recession](/wiki/1970s_recession "1970s recession") crippled traditional industries such as manufacturing in the New York City region. A rare positive highlight of the period was the completion of the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)"), a massive office complex in New York's [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District "Financial District") whose iconic, 110-story [Twin Towers](/wiki/Twin_Towers "Twin Towers") for a short time stood as the world's tallest buildings.

In the 1980s, the city's economy was booming, particularly in the financial sector. [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") was fueling an economic surge in the real estate market, and later the [dot-com bubble](/wiki/Dot-com_bubble "Dot-com bubble"). Despite this, crime was still an issue. This was exacerbated by the [crack epidemic](/wiki/Crack_epidemic "Crack epidemic"), with the New York City area being one of the major ports of entry for narcotics entering the United States. Neighborhoods such as the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx") became prime examples of late 20th century [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay"). Beginning in the 1990s, however, crime dropped substantially due to [tough-on-crime](/wiki/Tough-on-crime "Tough-on-crime") policies. [Crime in New York City](/wiki/Crime_in_New_York_City "Crime in New York City") has continued to decline through the 21st century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/WTC_smoking_on_9-11.jpeg/250px-WTC_smoking_on_9-11.jpeg)](/wiki/File:WTC_smoking_on_9-11.jpeg)

The [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") during the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in 2001, which caused nearly 3,000 deaths, most of whom were residents of the New York metropolitan region.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/One_World_Trade_Center_Building_%282021%29.jpg/120px-One_World_Trade_Center_Building_%282021%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_World_Trade_Center_Building_(2021).jpg)

[One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center"), built to replace the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973-2001) "World Trade Center (1973-2001)"), and opened in 2014

The [September 11th attacks](/wiki/September_11th_attacks "September 11th attacks") in 2001 were pivotal in the region and nation's history. The attacks killed nearly 3,000 people as two planes crashed into the former World Trade Center and caused the towers to collapse. Businesses led an exodus from Lower Manhattan because of this but were replaced by an increased number of [high-rise](/wiki/High-rise "High-rise") residences and a building boom in New York continues to this day.

In 2003, another blackout occurred, the [2003 North America blackout](/wiki/2003_North_America_blackout "2003 North America blackout"), but the city suffered no looting.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG/250px-Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hurricane_Sandy_Flooding_Avenue_C_2012.JPG)

A flooded [Avenue C](/wiki/Avenue_C_(Manhattan) "Avenue C (Manhattan)") in Manhattan just moments before the explosion at an [electrical substation](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation")

On October 29 and 30, 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") caused extensive destruction in the metropolitan area, ravaging portions of the Atlantic coastline with record-high [storm surge](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge"), severe flooding, and high winds, causing [power outages](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage") for millions of residents via downed trees and power lines and malfunctions at [electrical substations](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation"), leading to [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") shortages and snarling [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") systems. Damage to New York and New Jersey in terms of [physical infrastructure](/wiki/Physical_infrastructure "Physical infrastructure") and [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") as well as including interrupted commerce was estimated at several tens of billions of dollars.[[119]](#cite_note-122) The storm and its profound impacts have prompted the discussion of constructing [seawalls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall") and other coastal barriers around the shorelines of the metropolitan area to minimize the risk of destructive consequences from another such event in the future.[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-124)

[One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center"), also known as Freedom Tower, was completed in 2014 to replace the fallen Twin Towers.

The [2017–2021 New York City transit crisis](/wiki/2017%E2%80%932021_New_York_City_transit_crisis "2017–2021 New York City transit crisis"), which began with what media outlets referred to as the 2017 "summer of hell," led New York Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") to declare a state of emergency. The crisis was due to the interaction of multiple severe problems in the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") system and in [New York City bus lines](/wiki/MTA_Regional_Bus_Operations "MTA Regional Bus Operations"), as well as at [Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station"), the final stop on several of the commuter lines connecting New York City with other parts of the metropolitan area. Its root causes included long-term neglect of critical infrastructure and lack of adequate funding for ongoing operations, among others. The state of emergency was formally ended on June 30, 2021.

### Statistical history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Statistical history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg/250px-Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)

[Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan") on the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side") of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), c. 1900

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") first designated metropolitan areas in 1950 as [standard metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "Metropolitan Statistical Area") (SMAs). The "New York–Northeastern NJ SMA" was defined to include 17 counties: 9 in New York (the five boroughs of New York City, Nassau, Suffolk, Westchester, and Rockland) and 8 in New Jersey (Bergen, Hudson, Passaic, Essex, Union, Morris, Somerset, and Middlesex). In 1960, the metropolitan area standards were modified and renamed standard metropolitan statistical areas (SMSAs). The new standards resulted in the splitting of the former SMA into several pieces: the nine New York counties became the "New York SMSA"; three of the New Jersey counties (Essex, Union, and Morris) became the "Newark SMSA"; two other New Jersey counties (Bergen and Passaic) became the "Paterson–Passaic–Clifton SMSA"; Hudson County was designated the "Jersey City SMSA"; and Middlesex and Somerset counties lost their metropolitan status. In 1973, a new set of metropolitan area standards resulted in further changes: Nassau and Suffolk counties were split off as their own SMSA ("Nassau–Suffolk SMSA"); Bergen County (originally part of the Paterson–Clifton–Passaic SMSA) was transferred to the New York SMSA; the New York SMSA also received Putnam County (previously non-metropolitan); Somerset County was added to the Newark SMSA; and two new SMSAs, the "New Brunswick–Perth Amboy–Sayreville SMSA" (Middlesex County) and "Long Branch–Asbury Park SMSA" (Monmouth County), were established. In 1983, the concept of a consolidated metropolitan statistical area (CMSA) was first implemented. A CMSA consisted of several primary metropolitan statistical areas (PMSAs), which were individual employment centers within a wider [labor market area](/wiki/Labor_market_area "Labor market area"). The "New York–Northern New Jersey–Long Island CMSA" consisted of 12 PMSAs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Unisfera_Flushing.jpg/250px-Unisfera_Flushing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unisfera_Flushing.jpg)

[Unisphere](/wiki/Unisphere "Unisphere") in [Flushing Meadows – Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows_%E2%80%93_Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows – Corona Park"), iconic of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") [U.S. county](/wiki/U.S._county "U.S. county") and a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of New York[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[123]](#cite_note-126)

Seven PMSAs were based on the original 1950 New York SMA that were split up: New York, Bergen–Passaic, Jersey City, Middlesex–Somerset–Hunterdon (Hunterdon added for the first time), Monmouth–Ocean (Ocean added for the first time), Nassau–Suffolk, and Newark (Sussex added for the first time). One additional PMSA was the Orange County PMSA (previously the Newburgh–Middletown SMSA). The other four PMSAs were former SMSAs in Connecticut: Bridgeport, Stamford, Norwalk, and Danbury. In 1993, four PMSAs were added to the New York–Northern New Jersey–Long Island CMSA: Trenton PMSA (Mercer County), Dutchess County PMSA, Waterbury PMSA, and New Haven PMSA. Several new counties were also added to the CMSA: Sussex, Warren, and Pike. The CMSA model was originally utilized for tabulating data from the 2000 census. In 2003, a new set of standards was established using the [Core Based Statistical Area (CBSA)](/wiki/Core_based_statistical_area "Core based statistical area") model was adopted and remains in use as of 2010. The CBSA model resulted in the splitting up of the old CMSA into several metropolitan statistical areas: New York–Northern New Jersey–Long Island, Poughkeepsie–Newburgh–Middletown, Trenton–Princeton, Bridgeport–Stamford–Norwalk (includes Danbury), and New Haven–Milford (includes Waterbury). In 2013, the Census Bureau added Carbon, Lehigh, Northampton, and Monroe counties in Pennsylvania, and Warren County, New Jersey (encompassing collectively the Allentown-Bethlehem-Easton, PA-NJ MSA and the East Stroudsburg, PA MSA), to the Combined Statistical Area,[[124]](#cite_note-127) and assimilated Poughkeepsie–Newburgh–Middletown into the larger New York–Northern New Jersey–Long Island–NY–NJ–PA MSA. In 2018, the Allentown-Bethlehem-Easton, PA-NJ MSA was removed from the Combined Statistical Area.[[125]](#cite_note-OMB_20180914-128)

The July 2023 revision of the New York–Newark, NY–NJ–CT–PA CSA had the following municipality changes from the March 2020 definitions:[[126]](#cite_note-129)

Additions to the CSA:

* [Sullivan County, NY](/wiki/Sullivan_County,_New_York "Sullivan County, New York") (Monticello, NY μSA)

Removals from the CSA:

* [City of Shelton, CT](/wiki/Shelton,_Connecticut "Shelton, Connecticut") (from the erstwhile Bridgeport–Stamford–Norwalk, CT MSA)
* [Monroe County, PA](/wiki/Monroe_County,_Pennsylvania "Monroe County, Pennsylvania") (from the erstwhile East Stroudsburg, PA MSA)
* [New Haven County, CT](/wiki/New_Haven_County,_Connecticut "New Haven County, Connecticut") (from the erstwhile New Haven–Milford, CT MSA)
* [Litchfield County, CT](/wiki/Litchfield_County,_Connecticut "Litchfield County, Connecticut") (from the Torrington, CT μSA), except for the Towns of [Bridgewater](/wiki/Bridgewater,_Connecticut "Bridgewater, Connecticut") and [New Milford](/wiki/New_Milford,_Connecticut "New Milford, Connecticut")

### Proposals for the region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Proposals for the region")]

See also: [Partition and secession in New York](/wiki/Partition_and_secession_in_New_York "Partition and secession in New York")

The metropolitan region has never had separate political representation from the rest of their original states. This has to do with disagreements in the desired model and the constitutional complexity of the metropolitan region being cross-state. Within the State of New York over the last 30 years,[[127]](#cite_note-130) discussions have emerged of splitting the states into different regions with separate governors and legislators whilst remaining part of the same state — as opposed to seeing New York and its metropolitan area being split into a separate state.[[128]](#cite_note-SY-2Regmodle-131)[[129]](#cite_note-WKTV-3reg-132) The idea has been seen by Republicans in the state as an opportunity to dislocate the Democratic party's hold in the state legislature.[[130]](#cite_note-Fox-3reg-133)[[131]](#cite_note-Linge-134)

The discussion surrounding the re-organization of New York State has commonly been in two models: The two-region model creates a "downstate" New York region which would consist of all five New York City boroughs, Long Island's Nassau and Suffolk counties, and Westchester and Rockland counties, then Upstate would be the remaining 53;[[130]](#cite_note-Fox-3reg-133)[[128]](#cite_note-SY-2Regmodle-131) and the three-region model is New York having five counties; Montauk would consist of Nassau, Suffolk, Rockland, and Westchester counties and; New Amsterdam would be the remaining portion of New York State.[[130]](#cite_note-Fox-3reg-133)[[129]](#cite_note-WKTV-3reg-132)[[131]](#cite_note-Linge-134) This debate was reported as recent as February 2019, when Republican [state Senator](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate") [Daphne Jordan](/wiki/Daphne_Jordan "Daphne Jordan") supported the state being split into two states;[[130]](#cite_note-Fox-3reg-133)[[128]](#cite_note-SY-2Regmodle-131) however, it was believed that the proposal would require an act of congress for it to be passed.[[132]](#cite_note-135)

As of the 2010 Census, the metropolitan area had a population of 22,085,649. The population density was 1,865 per square mile. The racial markup was 51.7% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") (non-Latino), 21.7% [Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), 15.3% [African-American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 9.0% [Asian-American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.16% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and [Alaskan Native](/wiki/Alaskan_Native "Alaskan Native"), 0.03% [Pacific Islands American](/wiki/Pacific_Islands_American "Pacific Islands American"), 0.5% Other, and 1.6% [Multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial "Multiracial").[[140]](#cite_note-143)

The median age was 37.9. 25.5% were under 18, 9.5% were 18 to 24 years, 28% were 25 to 44 years of age, 26.6% were 45 to 64 years old, and 13.2% were over the age of 65. Males composed 48.3% of the population while females were 51.7% of the population.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

97.7% of the population were in households, 2.3% were in group quarters, and 1% were institutionalized. There were 8,103,731 households, of which 30.2% or 2,449,343 had children. 46.1% or 3,736,165 were composed of opposite sex and married couples. Male households with no wife composed 4.9% or 400,534. 15.0% or 1,212,436 were female households with no husbands. 34% or 2,754,596 were non-family households. The household density was 684 per square mile. 91.9% of housing units were occupied with a 3.8% vacancy rate. The average household size was 2.65 per household. The average income for non-family households was $90,335, and the average income for families was $104,715. 13.3% or 2,888,493 of the population were below the poverty line.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

26.7% or 5,911,993 of the population were born outside the United States. Out of this, most (50.6% or 2,992,639) were born in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), 27.0% or 1,595,523 were born in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), 17.4% or 1,028,506 were born in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), 3.8% or 224,109 were born in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and 0.2% or 11,957 were born in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Population estimates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Population estimates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Rockefeller_Center_Christmas_Tree_03.jpg/250px-Rockefeller_Center_Christmas_Tree_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rockefeller_Center_Christmas_Tree_03.jpg)

The [Rockefeller Center Christmas Tree](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center_Christmas_Tree "Rockefeller Center Christmas Tree"), considered the "worldwide symbol of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")", is an annual staple of the New York metropolitan area during the [Holiday season](/wiki/Christmas_and_holiday_season "Christmas and holiday season")[[141]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/New_York_Gay_Pride_2011.jpg/250px-New_York_Gay_Pride_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_Gay_Pride_2011.jpg)

The New York metropolitan area is home to the largest gay and bisexual community in the United States and one of the world's largest.[[142]](#cite_note-GLB-145)[[143]](#cite_note-WorldLargestGLBT-146)

As of 2020, the United States Census Bureau estimated the population of the [New York combined statistical area](/wiki/List_of_Combined_Statistical_Areas "List of Combined Statistical Areas") at 23,582,649, the most populous in the United States and one of the world's most populous [urban agglomerations](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration"). The increase in the population of the combined statistical area was distributed across the portions of the states of New York, New Jersey, Connecticut, and Pennsylvania which together constitute the greater New York City metropolitan area.

The New York metropolitan region is ethnically diverse. [Asian Americans in New York City](/wiki/Asian_Americans_in_New_York_City "Asian Americans in New York City"), according to the 2010 Census, number more than one million, greater than the combined totals of [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") and Los Angeles.[[144]](#cite_note-147) New York contains the highest total Asian population of any U.S. city proper.[[145]](#cite_note-148) The New York borough of Queens is home to the state's largest Asian American population and the largest [Andean](/wiki/Andes "Andes") ([Colombian](/wiki/Colombian_American "Colombian American"), [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian_American "Ecuadorian American"), [Peruvian](/wiki/Peruvian_American "Peruvian American"), [Chilean](/wiki/Chilean_American "Chilean American") and [Bolivian](/wiki/Bolivian_American "Bolivian American")) populations in the United States, and is also the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world.[[146]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld1-149)[[147]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld2-150) The [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") population constitutes the fastest-growing ethnicity in New York State; multiple [satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") of the original [Manhattan Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") (紐約華埠), in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Chinatown,_Brooklyn "Chinatown, Brooklyn") (布鲁克林華埠), and around [Flushing, Queens](/wiki/Chinatown,_Flushing "Chinatown, Flushing") (法拉盛華埠), are thriving as traditionally urban enclaves, while also expanding rapidly eastward into suburban [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York").[[148]](#cite_note-151) on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"),[[149]](#cite_note-152) as the New York metropolitan region and New York State have become the top destinations for new Chinese immigrants, respectively, and large-scale [Chinese immigration](/wiki/Chinese_emigration "Chinese emigration") continues into New York City and surrounding areas.[[150]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-153)[[151]](#cite_note-Chinese2013est-154)[[152]](#cite_note-155)[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157)[[155]](#cite_note-158) In 2012, 6.3% of New York was of [Chinese ethnicity](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American"), with nearly three-fourths living in either Queens or Brooklyn, geographically on Long Island.[[156]](#cite_note-159) In particular, the New York area has over 100,000 [Fuzhounese people](/wiki/Fuzhou_Americans "Fuzhou Americans").[[157]](#cite_note-160) A community numbering 20,000 [Korean-Chinese](/wiki/Koreans_in_China "Koreans in China") (*Chaoxianzu* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 朝鲜族) or *Joseonjok* ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 조선족)) is centered in [Flushing, Queens](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens"), while New York is also home to the largest [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") population outside China, India, and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), also centered in Queens.[[158]](#cite_note-UnreachedNY-161) [Koreans](/wiki/Korean_Americans_in_New_York_City "Korean Americans in New York City") made up 1.2% of the city's population, and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_in_New_York_City "Japanese in New York City") 0.3%. [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Filipinos in the New York City metropolitan region") were the largest [Southeast Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asian "Southeast Asian") ethnic group at 0.8%, followed by [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_American "Vietnamese American"), who made up 0.2% of New York's population in 2010. [Indians](/wiki/Indian_American "Indian American") are the largest [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian "South Asian") group, accounting for 2.4% of the city's population, with Bangladeshis and [Pakistanis](/wiki/Pakistani_American "Pakistani American") at 0.7% and 0.5%, respectively.[[159]](#cite_note-162) Queens is the preferred borough of settlement for Asian Indians, Koreans, and Filipinos,[[160]](#cite_note-163) as well as [Malaysians](/wiki/Malaysian_American "Malaysian American")[[19]](#cite_note-Immigrants2013est-19) and other Southeast Asians;[[161]](#cite_note-164) while Brooklyn is receiving large numbers of both [West Indian](/wiki/West_Indian_American "West Indian American")[[162]](#cite_note-165) as well as Asian Indian immigrants.

New York has the largest [European](/wiki/European_American "European American") and [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") population of any American city. At 2.7 million in 2012, New York's non-Hispanic white population is larger than the non-Hispanic white populations of Los Angeles (1.1 million), Chicago (865,000), and Houston (550,000) combined.[[163]](#cite_note-166) The [European diaspora](/wiki/European_diaspora "European diaspora") residing in the city is very diverse. According to 2012 Census estimates, there were roughly 560,000 [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), 385,000 [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"), 253,000 [German Americans](/wiki/German_American "German American"), 223,000 [Russian Americans](/wiki/Russian_Americans_in_New_York_City "Russian Americans in New York City"), 201,000 [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American"), and 137,000 [English Americans](/wiki/English_American "English American"). Additionally, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_American "Greek American") and [French Americans](/wiki/French_American "French American") numbered 65,000 each, with those of [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_American "Hungarian American") descent estimated at 60,000 people. [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_American "Ukrainian American") and [Scottish Americans](/wiki/Scottish_American "Scottish American") numbered 55,000 and 35,000, respectively. People identifying ancestry from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") numbered 30,838 total in 2010.[[164]](#cite_note-HispanicLatino-167) People of [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_American "Norwegian American") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_American "Swedish American") descent both stood at about 20,000 each, while people of [Czech](/wiki/Czech_American "Czech American"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_American "Lithuanian American"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_American "Portuguese American"), [Scotch-Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_American "Scotch-Irish American"), and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_American "Welsh American") descent all numbered between 12,000 and 14,000 people.[[165]](#cite_note-168) [Arab Americans](/wiki/Arab_American "Arab American") number over 160,000 in New York City,[[166]](#cite_note-169) with the highest concentration in Brooklyn. [Central Asians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Central_Asia "Demographics of Central Asia"), primarily [Uzbek Americans](/wiki/Uzbek_American "Uzbek American"), are a rapidly growing segment of the city's non-Hispanic white population, enumerating over 30,000, and including over half of all Central Asian immigrants to the United States,[[167]](#cite_note-170) most settling in Queens or Brooklyn. [Albanian Americans](/wiki/Albanian_American "Albanian American") are most highly concentrated in the Bronx.[[168]](#cite_note-171)

The wider New York metropolitan area is also [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity").[[169]](#cite_note-172) The New York metropolitan area is home the largest [African American/Black](/wiki/African_American "African American") population in the nation with nearly four million.[[170]](#cite_note-173) The New York region continues to be by far the leading metropolitan gateway for legal immigrants admitted into the United States, substantially exceeding the combined totals of Los Angeles and [Miami](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area"), the next most popular gateway regions.[[171]](#cite_note-174)[[172]](#cite_note-175)[[173]](#cite_note-176)[[174]](#cite_note-177) It is home to the largest [Jewish](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City") as well as [Israeli](/wiki/Israeli_American "Israeli American") communities outside [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), with the Jewish population in the region numbering over 1.5 million in 2012 and including many diverse Jewish sects from around the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[158]](#cite_note-UnreachedNY-161) The metropolitan area is also home to 20% of the nation's [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Indians in the New York City metropolitan region") and at least 20 [Little India](/wiki/Little_India_(location) "Little India (location)") enclaves, as well as 15% of all [Korean Americans](/wiki/Korean_Americans_in_New_York_City "Korean Americans in New York City") and four [Koreatowns](/wiki/Koreatown,_Manhattan "Koreatown, Manhattan");[[175]](#cite_note-factfinder2.census-178)[[176]](#cite_note-179) the largest [Asian Indian](/wiki/Asian_Indian "Asian Indian") population in the Western Hemisphere; the largest Russian American,[[150]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-153) [Italian American](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), and [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") populations; the largest [Dominican American](/wiki/Dominican_American "Dominican American"), [Puerto Rican American](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_migration_to_New_York_City "Puerto Rican migration to New York City"), and [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America")[[150]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-153) and second-largest overall [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_American "Hispanic and Latino American") population in the United States, numbering 4.8 million;[[164]](#cite_note-HispanicLatino-167) and includes at least 6 established [Chinatowns](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown") within New York City alone,[[177]](#cite_note-180) with the urban agglomeration consisting of a population of 819,527 uniracial [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans_in_New_York_City "Chinese Americans in New York City") as of 2014 Census estimates,[[178]](#cite_note-181) the largest outside of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[137]](#cite_note-NYCLargestChinesePopulation-140)[[138]](#cite_note-queensbuzz-141)

Ecuador, [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), Guyana, [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") were the top source countries from South America for legal immigrants to the New York region in 2013; the Dominican Republic, [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), Haiti, and Trinidad and Tobago in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbeans_in_New_York_City "Caribbeans in New York City"); [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") from [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"); and [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"), and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America").[[179]](#cite_note-182) Amidst a resurgence of [Puerto Rican migration to New York City](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_migration_to_New_York_City "Puerto Rican migration to New York City"), this population had increased to approximately 1.3 million in the metropolitan area as of 2013.

New York City has been described as the [gay capital](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City") of the world, and is home to one of the world's largest LGBTQ populations and the most prominent.[[180]](#cite_note-NYCGayCapitalOfTheWorld1-183) The New York metropolitan area is home to a self-identifying [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay") and [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexuality "Bisexuality") community estimated at 568,903 individuals, the largest in the United States and one of the world's largest.[[142]](#cite_note-GLB-145)[[143]](#cite_note-WorldLargestGLBT-146) [Same-sex marriages in New York](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York") were legalized on June 24, 2011, and were authorized to take place beginning 30 days thereafter.[[181]](#cite_note-184) The annual [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March") (or Gay [Pride Parade](/wiki/Pride_Parade "Pride Parade")) traverses southward down [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") in Manhattan, ending at [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), and is the largest pride parade in the world, attracting tens of thousands of participants and millions of sidewalk spectators each June.[[182]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-185)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/St._Patricks_Cathedral_%28New_York%29_%2845190701122%29.jpg/120px-St._Patricks_Cathedral_%28New_York%29_%2845190701122%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Patricks_Cathedral_(New_York)_(45190701122).jpg)

The [landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks "New York City Landmarks") [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_revival_architecture "Gothic revival architecture") [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [St. Patrick's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_(Manhattan) "St. Patrick's Cathedral (Manhattan)"), Midtown Manhattan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Jueus_ultraortodoxes_satmar_a_brooklyn.jpg/250px-Jueus_ultraortodoxes_satmar_a_brooklyn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jueus_ultraortodoxes_satmar_a_brooklyn.jpg)

Of Brooklyn's 2.4 million residents, 561,000 - or 23% - are Jewish as of 2011.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG/250px-Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG)

The [Islamic Cultural Center of New York](/wiki/Islamic_Cultural_Center_of_New_York "Islamic Cultural Center of New York") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): المركز الثقافي الإسلامي في نيويورك) in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"). With an estimated 1.5 million observers, the New York metropolitan area is home to the largest metropolitan [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_New_York_City "Islam in New York City") population in the Western Hemisphere.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Temple_at_Flushing%2C_NY.jpg/250px-Temple_at_Flushing%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temple_at_Flushing,_NY.jpg)

[Sri Maha Vallabha](/wiki/Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America "Hindu Temple Society of North America") [Ganapati](/wiki/Ganapati "Ganapati") [Devasthanam](/wiki/Devasthanam "Devasthanam") ([Kannada](/w/index.php?title=Kannada_Americans&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kannada Americans (page does not exist)"): ಶ್ರೀ ಮಹಾವಲ್ಲಭ ಗಣಪತಿ ದೇವಸ್ಥಾನ) or ([Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_American "Tamil American"): ஸ்ரீ மகா வல்லப கணபதி தேவஸ்தானம்), in [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), the oldest [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") temple in the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Chuang_Yen_Monastery_2004.jpeg/250px-Chuang_Yen_Monastery_2004.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Chuang_Yen_Monastery_2004.jpeg)

The [Chuang Yen Monastery](/wiki/Chuang_Yen_Monastery "Chuang Yen Monastery") (莊嚴寺), in [Kent](/wiki/Kent,_New_York "Kent, New York"), [Putnam County](/wiki/Putnam_County,_New_York "Putnam County, New York"), houses the largest indoor statue of [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[184]](#cite_note-187)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg/250px-Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory.jpg)

[Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory](/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory") on the [North Shore](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(Long_Island) "Gold Coast (Long Island)") of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), an internationally renowned [biomedical research](/wiki/Biomedical_research "Biomedical research") facility and home to eight scientists awarded the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine")

The New York City regional economy is the largest in the world, with a GDP of US$2.5 trillion in 2022, which would rank 8th among sovereign countries. Many [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") corporations are [headquartered](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") in New York,[[186]](#cite_note-189) as are a large number of [foreign corporations](/wiki/Foreign_corporation "Foreign corporation"). One out of ten [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") jobs in the city is with a foreign company.[[187]](#cite_note-190) In 2012 and 2015, New York topped the first and second *Global Economic Power Index* lists, respectively, as published by *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")*, with cities ranked according to criteria reflecting their presence on five different lists as published by five separate entities.[[31]](#cite_note-EconomicallyPowerful2015-31)[[188]](#cite_note-191) Finance, international trade, new and traditional media, real estate, education, fashion and entertainment, tourism, biotechnology, and manufacturing are the leading industries in the area. Along with its wealth, the area has a [cost of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living") that is the highest in the United States.

### Wall Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Wall Street")]

Main article: [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg/250px-Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photos_NewYork1_032.jpg)

[NYSE](/wiki/NYSE "NYSE") on [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), the world's largest stock market by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of listed companies.[[189]](#cite_note-NYSElargest-192)[[190]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-193)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Flatiron_District.jpg/250px-Flatiron_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flatiron_District.jpg)

Manhattan's [Flatiron District](/wiki/Flatiron_District "Flatiron District") was the cradle of Silicon Alley, now [metonymous](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") for the New York metropolitan region's [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") sector, which has since expanded beyond the area.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

The New York metropolitan area's most important economic sector lies in its role as the [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") for the [U.S. financial industry](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center"), metonymously known as *[Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street")*. Anchored by Wall Street, in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") of [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), New York has been called both the most economically powerful city and the leading financial center of the world,[[31]](#cite_note-EconomicallyPowerful2015-31)[[192]](#cite_note-195)[[193]](#cite_note-196)[[194]](#cite_note-197)[[195]](#cite_note-198) and the city is home to the world's two [largest stock exchanges](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges") by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization"), the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") and [NASDAQ](/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ").[[189]](#cite_note-NYSElargest-192)[[190]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-193) The city's [securities](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)") industry, enumerating 163,400 jobs in August 2013, continues to form the largest segment of the city's financial sector and an important economic engine, accounting in 2012 for 5 percent of the city's [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") jobs, 8.5 percent (US$3.8 billion) of its tax revenue, and 22 percent of the city's total wages, including an average salary of US$360,700.[[196]](#cite_note-199)

Manhattan had approximately 520 million square feet (48.1 million m2) of office space in 2013,[[197]](#cite_note-200) making it the largest office market in the United States,[[198]](#cite_note-201) while [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") is the largest [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") in the nation.[[199]](#cite_note-202)

[Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") is the third-largest central business district in the United States and is home to both the New York Stock Exchange, on Wall Street, and the [NASDAQ](/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ"), at [165 Broadway](/wiki/One_Liberty_Plaza "One Liberty Plaza"), representing the world's largest and second largest stock exchanges, respectively, when measured both by overall average daily trading volume and by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of their listed companies in 2013.[[190]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-193) Wall Street [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") fees in 2012 totaled approximately US$40 billion,[[200]](#cite_note-203) while in 2013, senior New York bank officers who manage [risk and compliance](/wiki/Risk_management "Risk management") functions earned as much as US$324,000 annually.[[201]](#cite_note-204)

In July 2013, [NYSE Euronext](/wiki/NYSE_Euronext "NYSE Euronext"), the operator of the New York Stock Exchange, took over the administration of the [London interbank offered rate](/wiki/London_interbank_offered_rate "London interbank offered rate") from the [British Bankers Association](/wiki/British_Bankers_Association "British Bankers Association").[[202]](#cite_note-205)

Many Wall Street firms have added or moved auxiliary financial or technical operations into [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), to take advantage of New Jersey's relatively lower [commercial real estate](/wiki/Commercial_real_estate "Commercial real estate") and rental prices, while offering continued geographic proximity to Manhattan's financial industry ecosystem.[[203]](#cite_note-206)

### Tech and biotech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Tech and biotech")]

Further information: [Tech:NYC](/wiki/Tech:NYC "Tech:NYC"), [Tech companies in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Tech_companies_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Tech companies in the New York metropolitan area"), [Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Biotech_and_pharmaceutical_companies_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in the New York metropolitan area"), and [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley")

Silicon Alley, centered in New York, has evolved into a [metonym](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") for the sphere encompassing the metropolitan region's [high technology](/wiki/High_technology "High technology") industries[[204]](#cite_note-207) involving the [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media"), [financial technology](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") (*fintech*) and [cryptocurrency](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency"), [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), [digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media"), [software development](/wiki/Software_development "Software development"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [game design](/wiki/Game_design "Game design"), and other fields within [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") that are supported by its [entrepreneurship ecosystem](/wiki/Entrepreneurship_ecosystem "Entrepreneurship ecosystem") and [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") investments. High technology [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_companies "Startup companies") and employment are growing in New York and across the metropolitan region, bolstered by the city's emergence as a global node of [creativity](/wiki/Innovation "Innovation") and [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship"), [social tolerance](/wiki/Social_tolerance "Social tolerance"),[[205]](#cite_note-SocialToleranceNY1-208) and [environmental sustainability](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability"),[[206]](#cite_note-EnvironmentalSustainabilityNY1-209)[[207]](#cite_note-EnvironmentalSustainabilityNY2-210) as well as New York's position as the leading Internet hub and telecommunications center in North America, including its vicinity to several [transatlantic fiber optic trunk lines](/wiki/Transatlantic_telephone_cable "Transatlantic telephone cable"),[[208]](#cite_note-211) the city's [intellectual capital](/wiki/Intellectual_capital "Intellectual capital"), and its extensive outdoor [wireless connectivity](/wiki/Wireless_network "Wireless network").[[209]](#cite_note-212) [Verizon Communications](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications"), headquartered at [140 West Street](/wiki/Barclay-Vesey_Building "Barclay-Vesey Building") in Lower Manhattan, was at the final stages in 2014 of completing a US$3 billion [fiberoptic telecommunications](/wiki/Fiberoptic_communication "Fiberoptic communication") upgrade throughout New York City.[[210]](#cite_note-213)

The biotechnology sector is also growing in the New York metropolitan region, based upon its strength in academic scientific [research](/wiki/Research "Research") and public and commercial financial support. On December 19, 2011, then-New York mayor [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") announced his choice of [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") and [Technion-Israel Institute of Technology](/wiki/Technion-Israel_Institute_of_Technology "Technion-Israel Institute of Technology") to build [Cornell Tech](/wiki/Cornell_Tech "Cornell Tech"), a US$2 billion [graduate school](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school") of [applied sciences](/wiki/Applied_science "Applied science") on [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island"), Manhattan with the goal of transforming New York into the world's premier technology capital.[[211]](#cite_note-214)[[212]](#cite_note-215) By mid-2014, Accelerator, a biotech [investment](/wiki/Investment "Investment") firm, had raised more than US$30 million from [investors](/wiki/Investor "Investor"), including [Eli Lilly and Company](/wiki/Eli_Lilly_and_Company "Eli Lilly and Company"), [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer"), and [Johnson & Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_%26_Johnson "Johnson & Johnson"), for initial funding to create biotechnology [startups](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") at the Alexandria Center for Life Science, which encompasses more than 700,000 square feet (65,000 m2) on [East 29th Street](/wiki/29th_Street_(Manhattan) "29th Street (Manhattan)") and promotes collaboration among scientists and [entrepreneurs](/wiki/Entrepreneur "Entrepreneur") at the center and with nearby academic, medical, and research institutions. The [New York City Economic Development Corporation](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation")'s Early Stage Life Sciences Funding Initiative and venture capital partners, including [Celgene](/wiki/Celgene "Celgene"), [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") Ventures, and Eli Lilly, committed a minimum of US$100 million to help launch 15 to 20 ventures in [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences") and biotechnology.[[213]](#cite_note-216) [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County,_New_York "Westchester County, New York") has also developed a burgeoning biotechnology sector in the 21st century, with over US$1 billion in planned private investment as of 2016,[[214]](#cite_note-217) earning the county the nickname *Biochester*.[[215]](#cite_note-218)

### Port of New York and New Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Port of New York and New Jersey")]

Main article: [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Manhattan.jpg/250px-Manhattan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manhattan.jpg)

[Port Newark–Elizabeth Marine Terminal](/wiki/Port_Newark%E2%80%93Elizabeth_Marine_Terminal "Port Newark–Elizabeth Marine Terminal") on [Newark Bay](/wiki/Newark_Bay "Newark Bay") is the busiest container terminal on the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States").

The Port of New York and New Jersey is the [port district](/wiki/Port_district "Port district") of the New York metropolitan area, encompassing the region within approximately a 25-mile (40 km) radius of the [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument"). A major economic engine for the New York metropolitan area, the [port](/wiki/Port "Port") includes the system of navigable waterways in the [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") along 650 miles (1,050 km) of shoreline in the vicinity of New York and the [Gateway Region](/wiki/Gateway_Region "Gateway Region") of northeastern New Jersey, as well as the [region's airports](/wiki/Aviation_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Aviation in the New York metropolitan area") and supporting rail and roadway distribution networks. The Port of New York and New Jersey handled a [maritime](/wiki/Maritime_transport "Maritime transport") [cargo](/wiki/Cargo "Cargo") volume in the ten months through October 2022 of over 8.2 million [TEUs](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit"), benefitting [post-Panamax](/wiki/Panamax "Panamax") from the expansion of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal"), and accelerating ahead of California seaports in monthly cargo volumes.[[216]](#cite_note-219)[[217]](#cite_note-220)

### Water purity and availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Water purity and availability")]

Further information: [Food and water in New York City](/wiki/Food_and_water_in_New_York_City "Food and water in New York City") and [New York City water supply system](/wiki/New_York_City_water_supply_system "New York City water supply system")

Water purity and availability are a lifeline for the New York metropolitan region. New York City is supplied with drinking water by the protected [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin").[[218]](#cite_note-221) As a result of the watershed's integrity and undisturbed [natural water filtration system](/wiki/Water_purification "Water purification"), New York is one of only four major cities in the United States the majority of whose drinking water is pure enough not to require purification by [water treatment](/wiki/Water_treatment "Water treatment") plants.[[219]](#cite_note-222) The [Croton Watershed](/wiki/Croton_Falls_Reservoir "Croton Falls Reservoir") north of the city is undergoing construction of a US$3.2 billion water purification plant to augment New York's water supply by an estimated 290 million gallons daily, representing a greater than 20% addition to the city's current availability of water.[[220]](#cite_note-223) The ongoing expansion of [New York City Water Tunnel No. 3](/wiki/New_York_City_Water_Tunnel_No._3 "New York City Water Tunnel No. 3"), an integral part of the New York City water supply system, is the largest capital construction project in the city's history,[[221]](#cite_note-224) with segments serving Manhattan and The Bronx completed, and with segments serving Brooklyn and Queens planned for construction in 2020.[[222]](#cite_note-225) Much of the fresh water for northern and central New Jersey is provided by [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir"), but numerous municipal [water wells](/wiki/Water_well "Water well") exist which accomplish the same purpose.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Low_Library_on_campus._-_panoramio.jpg/330px-Low_Library_on_campus._-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Low_Library_on_campus._-_panoramio.jpg)

[Low Library](/wiki/Low_Library "Low Library"), the Neoclassical centerpiece of the [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") campus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Harkness_Tower%2C_high_contrast.jpg/250px-Harkness_Tower%2C_high_contrast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harkness_Tower,_high_contrast.jpg)

The bronze clock on [Harkness Tower](/wiki/Harkness_Tower "Harkness Tower") at [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), a structure reflecting the [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") architectural genre

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/ClevelandTowerWatercolor20060829.jpg/250px-ClevelandTowerWatercolor20060829.jpg)](/wiki/File:ClevelandTowerWatercolor20060829.jpg)

Watercolor of [Cleveland Tower](/wiki/Cleveland_Tower "Cleveland Tower"), [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), seen in the noon autumn sun

The New York metropolitan area is home to many prestigious institutions of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education"). Three [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") universities: [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in [Manhattan, New York City](/wiki/Manhattan,_New_York_City "Manhattan, New York City"); [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") in [Princeton, New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton,_New_Jersey "Princeton, New Jersey"); [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") in New Haven, Connecticut – all ranked amongst the top 3 U.S. national universities as per *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* as of 2018[[223]](#cite_note-USNewsrankings-226) – reside in the region,[[224]](#cite_note-227) as well as [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") and [The Rockefeller University](/wiki/The_Rockefeller_University "The Rockefeller University"), both located in Manhattan; all of the above have been ranked amongst the top 35 universities in the world.[[225]](#cite_note-228) [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University"), a global university located 27 mi (43 km) southwest of Manhattan in [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey") and [Piscataway, New Jersey](/wiki/Piscataway,_New_Jersey "Piscataway, New Jersey"), is by far the largest university in the region.[[226]](#cite_note-229) [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology") is located on two campuses, one in Old Westbury, [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") and one near Columbus Circle in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"). [Hofstra University](/wiki/Hofstra_University "Hofstra University") is Long Island's largest private university.[[227]](#cite_note-230) [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University"), also a Tier-1 university,[[228]](#cite_note-231) is the oldest [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") institution of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in the [northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"),[[229]](#cite_note-232) and the third-oldest university in [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").[[230]](#cite_note-233) The [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education") is the largest school district in the United States serving over 1.2 million students.[[231]](#cite_note-234) The overall region also hosts many public high schools, some of which have been described as among the most prestigious in the country.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

### Attainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Attainment")]

According to the 2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), of the 14,973,063 persons in this area over 25 years of age, 14.8% (2,216,578) had a graduate or professional degree, 21.1% (3,166,037) had a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), 6.4% (962,007) had an [associate degree](/wiki/Associate_degree "Associate degree"), 16.0% (2,393,990) had some college education but no degree, 26.8% (4,009,901) had a high school diploma or equivalent, 14.8% (2,224,557) had less than a high school education.[[233]](#cite_note-236) In 2010, CNN Money ranked the area as one of the top 10 smartest regions in the United States.[[234]](#cite_note-237)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg/250px-R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg)

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") is the world's largest [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system by length of routes and by number of [stations](/wiki/Train_station "Train station").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/PATH_Kawasaki_5602c.jpg/250px-PATH_Kawasaki_5602c.jpg)](/wiki/File:PATH_Kawasaki_5602c.jpg)

The [Port Authority Trans-Hudson (PATH)](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson") rapid transit rail system connects Manhattan and metropolitan [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") beneath the Hudson River.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Acela_Express_and_Metro-North_railcar.jpg/250px-Acela_Express_and_Metro-North_railcar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Acela_Express_and_Metro-North_railcar.jpg)

An [Acela Express](/wiki/Acela_Express "Acela Express") train going to New York. The Acela Express, operated by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") through the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor"), is the sole [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") service in the country.

The depth and intricacy of the transportation network in the New York region parallels the size and complexity of the metropolis itself.

In 2013, the New York-Newark-Jersey City metropolitan statistical area (New York MSA) had the lowest percentage of workers who commuted by private automobile (56.9 percent), with 18.9 percent of area workers traveling via rail transit. During the period starting in 2006 and ending in 2013, the New York MSA had a 2.2 percent decline of workers commuting by automobile.[[235]](#cite_note-238)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Rail")]

About one in every three users of [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") in the United States and two-thirds of the nation's rail riders live in the New York metropolitan area.[[236]](#cite_note-239)[[237]](#cite_note-240)

#### New York City Subway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: New York City Subway")]

Main article: [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") is the largest [rapid transit system](/wiki/Rapid_transit_system "Rapid transit system") in the world when measured by stations in operation, with 472, and by length of routes. In 2006 it was the third largest when measured by annual ridership (1.5 billion passenger trips in 2006),[[238]](#cite_note-241) However, in 2013, the subway delivered over 1.71 billion rides,[[239]](#cite_note-242) but slipped to being the [seventh busiest rapid transit rail system](/wiki/Metro_systems_by_annual_passenger_rides "Metro systems by annual passenger rides") in the world.[[240]](#cite_note-243) New York's subway is also notable because nearly the entire system remains open 24 hours a day, in contrast to the overnight shutdown common to systems in most cities, including [Hong Kong](/wiki/MTR "MTR"),[[241]](#cite_note-244)[[242]](#cite_note-245) [London](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul_Metropolitan_Subway "Seoul Metropolitan Subway"),[[243]](#cite_note-246)[[244]](#cite_note-247) [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo_Subway "Tokyo Subway"), and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto_Subway_and_RT "Toronto Subway and RT").[[245]](#cite_note-248)

#### PATH

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: PATH")]

Main article: [PATH (rail system)](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system) "PATH (rail system)")

[PATH](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system) "PATH (rail system)") is a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system connecting the cities of [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), [Harrison](/wiki/Harrison,_New_Jersey "Harrison, New Jersey"), [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), and [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), in metropolitan northern [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), with the [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") and [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") sections of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in New York City. The PATH is operated by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey"). PATH trains run 24 hours a day and 7 days a week.[[246]](#cite_note-PATH-Timetable-249) The system has a total route length of 13.8 mi (22.2 km), not double-counting route overlaps.[[247]](#cite_note-WTC-progress-250)

#### Commuter rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Commuter rail")]

The metropolitan area is also fundamentally defined by the areas from which people commute into New York. The city is served by three primary [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") systems, and is provided [intercity rail](/wiki/Intercity_rail "Intercity rail") transit with [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak").

The [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") (LIRR), the busiest [commuter railroad in the United States](/wiki/Commuter_rail_in_North_America "Commuter rail in North America") as of 2015,[[248]](#cite_note-APTA-2015-Q4-251) is operated by the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") (MTA), an agency of the [State Government of New York](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)") that focuses on New York City-area transit). It has two major terminals at Pennsylvania Station in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") and [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), with a minor terminal at the [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City_(LIRR_station) "Long Island City (LIRR station)") station and a major transfer point at the [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_(LIRR_station) "Jamaica (LIRR station)") station in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens").

[New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_Rail_Operations "New Jersey Transit Rail Operations") (NJT), the second busiest commuter railroad in the United States as of 2015,[[248]](#cite_note-APTA-2015-Q4-251) is operated by the New Jersey Transit Corporation, an agency of the state of New Jersey, in conjunction with Metro-North Railroad and Amtrak. It has major terminals at Pennsylvania Station in Manhattan, Hoboken Terminal, and [Newark Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(Newark) "Pennsylvania Station (Newark)"), with a major transfer point at [Secaucus Junction](/wiki/Secaucus_Junction "Secaucus Junction") in [Hudson County, New Jersey](/wiki/Hudson_County,_New_Jersey "Hudson County, New Jersey"). New Jersey Transit also operates the [Hudson–Bergen Light Rail](/wiki/Hudson%E2%80%93Bergen_Light_Rail "Hudson–Bergen Light Rail") through Hudson County, the [Newark Light Rail](/wiki/Newark_Light_Rail "Newark Light Rail"), and the [River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(New_Jersey_Transit) "River Line (New Jersey Transit)") that runs along tracks shared with [Conrail Shared Assets Operations](/wiki/Conrail_Shared_Assets_Operations "Conrail Shared Assets Operations") from Trenton to [Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"). NJ Transit also has commuter buses operating in and out of Manhattan.

[Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") (MNRR), the third busiest commuter railroad in the United States as of 2015,[[248]](#cite_note-APTA-2015-Q4-251) is also operated by the MTA, in conjunction with the [Connecticut Department of Transportation](/wiki/Connecticut_Department_of_Transportation "Connecticut Department of Transportation") and [New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_Rail_Operations "New Jersey Transit Rail Operations"). Its major terminal is [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"). Trains on the [Port Jervis Line](/wiki/Port_Jervis_Line "Port Jervis Line") and [Pascack Valley Line](/wiki/Pascack_Valley_Line "Pascack Valley Line") terminate at [Hoboken Terminal](/wiki/Hoboken_Terminal "Hoboken Terminal") in [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"); commuters may transfer at either [Secaucus Junction](/wiki/Secaucus_Junction "Secaucus Junction") for New Jersey Transit trains to [New York Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)") or at Hoboken Terminal for [PATH](/wiki/Port_Authority_Trans-Hudson "Port Authority Trans-Hudson") trains into Manhattan.

Amtrak's [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor") offers service to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), New Haven, and other points between and including [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

### Major highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Major highways")]

The following highways serve the region:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/George_Washington_Bridge%2C_on_a_late_winter_afternoon.jpg/250px-George_Washington_Bridge%2C_on_a_late_winter_afternoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Washington_Bridge,_on_a_late_winter_afternoon.jpg)

The [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge"), connecting [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan") in Upper Manhattan across the Hudson River to Fort Lee in Bergen County, New Jersey, is the world's busiest motor vehicle bridge.[[249]](#cite_note-panynj.gov-252)[[250]](#cite_note-abcgwb-253) [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95 "Interstate 95") and [U.S. Route 1/9](/wiki/U.S._Route_1/9 "U.S. Route 1/9") cross the river via the bridge, while [U.S. Route 46](/wiki/U.S._Route_46 "U.S. Route 46"), which lies entirely within New Jersey, ends halfway across the bridge at the state border with New York.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Walkway_Over_the_Hudson_4.JPG/250px-Walkway_Over_the_Hudson_4.JPG)](/wiki/File:Walkway_Over_the_Hudson_4.JPG)

The [Walkway over the Hudson](/wiki/Poughkeepsie_Bridge "Poughkeepsie Bridge"), the world's longest [pedestrian](/wiki/Pedestrian "Pedestrian") bridge,[[251]](#cite_note-guinnessworldrecords.com-254) connects [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_County "Ulster County") and [Dutchess](/wiki/Dutchess_County "Dutchess County") counties in New York.
