#### Named bridges and tunnels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Named bridges and tunnels")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Brooklyn_Bridge_NYC_August_16%2C_2010.JPG/250px-Brooklyn_Bridge_NYC_August_16%2C_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_NYC_August_16,_2010.JPG)

The [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") connects [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge_at_night.jpg/250px-Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge_at_night.jpg)

[Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge"), one of the world's longest [suspension bridges](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge"),[[252]](#cite_note-infoplease.com-255)[[253]](#cite_note-Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge-256) connects [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") across [The Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/GreatSouth-B-B.jpg/250px-GreatSouth-B-B.jpg)](/wiki/File:GreatSouth-B-B.jpg)

[Great South Bay Bridge](/wiki/Great_South_Bay_Bridge "Great South Bay Bridge"), in [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York"), connects [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") with the [barrier islands](/wiki/Barrier_island "Barrier island") across the [Great South Bay](/wiki/Great_South_Bay "Great South Bay").

* [Alexander Hamilton Bridge](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_Bridge "Alexander Hamilton Bridge") connecting the Trans-Manhattan Expressway in the Washington Heights section of Manhattan and the Cross-Bronx Expressway, as part of Interstate 95
* [Basilone Bridge](/wiki/Basilone_Bridge "Basilone Bridge") (part of I-95 and the New Jersey Turnpike)
* [Chaplain Washington–Harry Laderman Bridge](/wiki/Chaplain_Washington%E2%80%93Harry_Laderman_Bridge "Chaplain Washington–Harry Laderman Bridge") (part of I-95 and NJ Turnpike)
* [Bayonne Bridge](/wiki/Bayonne_Bridge "Bayonne Bridge") (part of NY 440 and NJ 440), underwent a $1 billion project to raise the roadway by 64 feet to 215 feet to allow taller [container ships](/wiki/Container_ship "Container ship") to pass underneath to access [seaports](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") in New York City and northern New Jersey.[[254]](#cite_note-257)
* [Bear Mountain Bridge](/wiki/Bear_Mountain_Bridge "Bear Mountain Bridge") (part of US 6 and US 202)
* [Bronx–Whitestone Bridge](/wiki/Bronx%E2%80%93Whitestone_Bridge "Bronx–Whitestone Bridge") (part of I-678) – connects the boroughs of Bronx and Queens.
* [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), iconic of New York and designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") by the [U.S. National Park Service](/wiki/U.S._National_Park_Service "U.S. National Park Service") on January 29, 1964.[[255]](#cite_note-258) Connects Brooklyn and lower Manhattan (at Park Row and City Hall).
* [Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel") (part of I-478), officially renamed the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel, in honor of the former New York State governor – connects Brooklyn and lower Manhattan (financial district).
* [Delaware Water Gap Toll Bridge](/wiki/Delaware_Water_Gap_Toll_Bridge "Delaware Water Gap Toll Bridge") (part of I-80 crossing the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"))
* [Driscoll Bridge](/wiki/Driscoll_Bridge "Driscoll Bridge") (part of the Garden State Parkway), with a total of 15 travel lanes and 6 shoulder lanes, the widest motor vehicle bridge in the world by number of lanes[[256]](#cite_note-TOLLROADSnews,_Peter_Samuel-259) and one of the world's busiest.
* [Ed Koch Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Ed_Koch_Queensboro_Bridge "Ed Koch Queensboro Bridge") (part of NY 25) – renamed in honor of former New York Mayor Edward I. Koch, also known informally as the "59th Street Bridge". Connects Queens and the east side of Manhattan.
* [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge") (part of I-95 and US 1-9/46), the world's busiest motor vehicle bridge[[249]](#cite_note-panynj.gov-252)[[250]](#cite_note-abcgwb-253) and one of the world's widest, with 14 lanes.[[256]](#cite_note-TOLLROADSnews,_Peter_Samuel-259)
* [Goethals Bridge](/wiki/Goethals_Bridge "Goethals Bridge") (part of I-278)
* [Great South Bay Bridge](/wiki/Great_South_Bay_Bridge "Great South Bay Bridge"), Long Island
* [Heroes Tunnel](/wiki/Heroes_Tunnel "Heroes Tunnel") (formerly the West Rock Tunnel) (part of CT 15)
* [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel") (part of I-78 and [NJ 139](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_139 "New Jersey Route 139"))
* [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel") (part of Route 495)
* [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), connecting Brooklyn to [Chinatown, Manhattan](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan"), carries four tracks of the [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)")​, [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)") trains of the New York City Subway, in addition to seven lanes of traffic.
* [Mid-Hudson Bridge](/wiki/Mid-Hudson_Bridge "Mid-Hudson Bridge") (part of US 44 and NY 55)
* [Newark Bay Bridge](/wiki/Newark_Bay_Bridge "Newark Bay Bridge") (part of I-78)
* [New Hope – Lambertville Toll Bridge](/wiki/New_Hope_%E2%80%93_Lambertville_Toll_Bridge "New Hope – Lambertville Toll Bridge") (part of US 202 crossing the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"))
* [Newburgh–Beacon Bridge](/wiki/Newburgh%E2%80%93Beacon_Bridge "Newburgh–Beacon Bridge") (part of I-84 and NY 52)
* [Otisville Tunnel](/wiki/Otisville_Tunnel "Otisville Tunnel") (takes the [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") [Port Jervis Line](/wiki/Port_Jervis_Line "Port Jervis Line") through the [Shawangunk Ridge](/wiki/Shawangunk_Ridge "Shawangunk Ridge") in [Orange County, New York](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York"))
* [Outerbridge Crossing](/wiki/Outerbridge_Crossing "Outerbridge Crossing") (part of NY 440 and NJ 440)
* [Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_Memorial_Bridge_(Connecticut) "Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge (Connecticut)") (part of I-95 and the Connecticut Turnpike)
* [Poughkeepsie Bridge](/wiki/Poughkeepsie_Bridge "Poughkeepsie Bridge"), also known as Walkway over the Hudson, the world's longest [pedestrian](/wiki/Pedestrian "Pedestrian") bridge,[[251]](#cite_note-guinnessworldrecords.com-254) connecting [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster_County "Ulster County") and [Dutchess](/wiki/Dutchess_County "Dutchess County") counties in New York
* [Pulaski Skyway](/wiki/Pulaski_Skyway "Pulaski Skyway") (part of US 1–9)
* [Queens–Midtown Tunnel](/wiki/Queens%E2%80%93Midtown_Tunnel "Queens–Midtown Tunnel") (part of I-495) – connects Queens and Midtown Manhattan.
* [Scudder Falls Bridge](/wiki/Scudder_Falls_Bridge "Scudder Falls Bridge") (part of I-295 crossing the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"))
* [Sikorsky Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Sikorsky_Memorial_Bridge "Sikorsky Memorial Bridge") (part of CT 15 Merritt & Wilbur Cross Parkways)
* [Tappan Zee Bridge](/wiki/Tappan_Zee_Bridge_(2017%E2%80%93present) "Tappan Zee Bridge (2017–present)") (part of I-87, I-287, and the New York State Thruway), the longest bridge in New York State; underwent a $4 billion replacement.[[257]](#cite_note-260)
* [Thomas Alva Edison Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Edison_Bridge_(New_Jersey) "Edison Bridge (New Jersey)") (part of US 9)
* [Throgs Neck Bridge](/wiki/Throgs_Neck_Bridge "Throgs Neck Bridge") (part of I-295) – connects the boroughs of Bronx and Queens (at the western end of Long Island Sound).
* [Trenton–Morrisville Toll Bridge](/wiki/Trenton%E2%80%93Morrisville_Toll_Bridge "Trenton–Morrisville Toll Bridge") (part of US 1)
* [Triborough Bridge](/wiki/Triborough_Bridge "Triborough Bridge") (part of I-278), officially renamed the Robert F. Kennedy (RFK) Bridge – connects the three boroughs of Manhattan, Bronx and Queens (hence its name).
* [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") (part of I-278), the longest [suspension bridge](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and one of the longest in the world (formerly the world's longest) – connects the boroughs of Staten Island and Brooklyn.[[252]](#cite_note-infoplease.com-255)[[253]](#cite_note-Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge-256)
* [William A. Stickel Memorial Bridge](/wiki/William_A._Stickel_Memorial_Bridge "William A. Stickel Memorial Bridge") (part of I-280)
* [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), carries 2 tracks of the [J](/wiki/J_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "J (New York City Subway service)"), [M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)"), and [Z](/wiki/Z_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Z (New York City Subway service)")​ trains of the New York City Subway, in addition to 8 lanes of traffic – connects [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn"), Brooklyn, and the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side") or Manhattan.

### Commuter bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Commuter bus")]

[New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit "New Jersey Transit"), [Academy Bus](/wiki/Academy_Bus "Academy Bus"), [Coach USA](/wiki/Coach_USA "Coach USA"), [Spanish Transportation](/wiki/Spanish_Transportation "Spanish Transportation"), [Trailways of New York](/wiki/Trailways_of_New_York "Trailways of New York"), and several other companies operate commuter coaches into the [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") in Manhattan, and many other bus services in New Jersey. Bus services also operate in other nearby counties in the states of New York and Connecticut, but most terminate at a subway terminal or other rail station.

### Commuter usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Commuter usage")]

According to the 2010 American Community Survey, 54.3% (5,476,169) of commuters used a car or other private vehicle alone, 7.0% (708,788) used a carpool, 27.0% (2,721,372) used public transportation, 5.5% (558,434) walked to work, 2.0% (200,448) used some other means of transportation such as a bicycle to get to work.[[258]](#cite_note-261)

## Culture and contemporary life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Culture and contemporary life")]

Main article: [New York City § Culture](/wiki/New_York_City#Culture "New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Metropolitan_museum_of_art_2.jpg/250px-Metropolitan_museum_of_art_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metropolitan_museum_of_art_2.jpg)

[Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), part of [Museum Mile](/wiki/Museum_Mile,_New_York_City "Museum Mile, New York City") in the [Carnegie Hill](/wiki/Carnegie_Hill "Carnegie Hill") neighborhood of Manhattan's [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side"), is one of the largest and most visited museums in the world.[[259]](#cite_note-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Citi_Field_2011.JPG/250px-Citi_Field_2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Citi_Field_2011.JPG)

[Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field") in [Flushing, Queens](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens"), home of the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg/250px-Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yankee_Stadium_upper_deck_2010.jpg)

[Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") in the [South Bronx](/wiki/South_Bronx "South Bronx"), home of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") and [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg/250px-New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Meadowlands_Stadium_Mezz_Corner.jpg)

[MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"), home to the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), was the most expensive stadium ever built,[[260]](#cite_note-Esteban-263) at approximately $1.6 billion.[[261]](#cite_note-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Tsq_green_chairs_jeh.jpg/250px-Tsq_green_chairs_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tsq_green_chairs_jeh.jpg)

*[Travel + Leisure](/wiki/Travel_%2B_Leisure "Travel + Leisure")* magazine's October 2011 survey named [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), iconified as the "Crossroads of the World",[[262]](#cite_note-265)[[263]](#cite_note-266)[[264]](#cite_note-267)[[265]](#cite_note-268)[[266]](#cite_note-269) the world's most visited tourist attraction with over 39 million visitors annually.[[267]](#cite_note-270)

New York has been described as the cultural capital of the world by the [diplomatic consulates](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")[[268]](#cite_note-271) and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia")[[269]](#cite_note-272) and by New York's own [Baruch College](/wiki/Baruch_College "Baruch College").[[270]](#cite_note-273) A book containing a series of essays titled *New York, culture capital of the world, 1940–1965* has also been published as showcased by the [National Library of Australia](/wiki/National_Library_of_Australia "National Library of Australia").[[271]](#cite_note-274) [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe") has quoted regarding New York's culture that "Culture just seems to be in the air, like part of the weather."[[272]](#cite_note-275)

Although Manhattan remains the epicenter of cultural life in the metropolitan area, the entire region is replete with prominent cultural institutions, with artistic performances and ethnically oriented events receiving international attention throughout the year.

### Sports teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Sports teams")]

Further information: [Sports in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Sports_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Sports in the New York metropolitan area")

See also: [Soccer in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Soccer_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Soccer in the New York metropolitan area")

The New York metropolitan area is home to the [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"),[[273]](#cite_note-276) [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"),[[274]](#cite_note-277) the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"),[[275]](#cite_note-278) the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"),[[276]](#cite_note-279) and [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer").[[277]](#cite_note-280) Four of the ten most expensive [stadiums](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") ever built worldwide ([MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), the new [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"), [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), and [Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field")) are located in the New York metropolitan area.[[260]](#cite_note-Esteban-263) The New York metropolitan area has the [highest total number of professional sports teams](/wiki/List_of_American_and_Canadian_cities_by_number_of_major_professional_sports_teams "List of American and Canadian cities by number of major professional sports teams") in these five leagues.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in New York City](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City")

The New York metropolitan area is home to the headquarters of several well-known media companies, subsidiaries, and publications, including [Thomson Reuters](/wiki/Thomson_Reuters "Thomson Reuters"), [The New York Times Company](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Company "The New York Times Company"), the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery"), [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), the [Hearst Corporation](/wiki/Hearst_Corporation "Hearst Corporation"), [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global"), [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp"), the [Fox Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Corporation "Fox Corporation"), [The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal"), [Fox News](/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News"), [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), and [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"). Local television channels broadcasting to the New York market include [WCBS-TV](/wiki/WCBS-TV "WCBS-TV") 2 (CBS), [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") 4 (NBC), [WNYW](/wiki/WNYW "WNYW") 5 (FOX), [WABC-TV](/wiki/WABC-TV "WABC-TV") 7 (ABC), [WWOR-TV](/wiki/WWOR-TV "WWOR-TV") 9 (MyNetworkTV), [WPIX](/wiki/WPIX "WPIX") 11 (CW), [WNET](/wiki/WNET "WNET") 13 (PBS), [WNYE-TV](/wiki/WNYE-TV "WNYE-TV") 25 (NYC Media) and [WPXN-TV](/wiki/WPXN-TV "WPXN-TV") 31 ([Ion](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television")). [NY1](/wiki/NY1 "NY1") is a 24/7 local news provider available only to [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") subscribers. Radio stations serving the area include: [WNYC](/wiki/WNYC_(AM) "WNYC (AM)"), [WKCR](/wiki/WKCR "WKCR"), [WFMU](/wiki/WFMU "WFMU"), [WABC](/wiki/WABC_(AM) "WABC (AM)"), and [WFAN](/wiki/WFAN_(AM) "WFAN (AM)"). Many television and radio stations use the top of the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") to broadcast their [terrestrial television](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") signals, while some media entities broadcast from studios in [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square").

The New York metropolitan area is extensive enough so that its own channels must compete with channels from neighboring television markets (including [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Scranton/Wilkes-Barre](/wiki/Wyoming_Valley "Wyoming Valley"), and [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford "Hartford")) within its outlying counties. Cable companies offer such competition in the Pennsylvania portion, Connecticut, and a few counties in central New Jersey.

#### In New Jersey

Skyline of [Six Flags Great Adventure](/wiki/Six_Flags_Great_Adventure "Six Flags Great Adventure") in [Jackson Township, New Jersey](/wiki/Jackson_Township,_New_Jersey "Jackson Township, New Jersey"), the world's largest [theme park](/wiki/Theme_park "Theme park")[[278]](#cite_note-281) To the far left is [Kingda Ka](/wiki/Kingda_Ka "Kingda Ka"), the world's tallest [roller coaster](/wiki/Roller_coaster "Roller coaster") until its closure in 2024.[[279]](#cite_note-282)

#### In New York State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: In New York State")]

[Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), in Brooklyn, is considered one of America's first amusement parks.

[Playland](/wiki/Playland_(New_York) "Playland (New York)"), in [Rye](/wiki/Rye,_New_York "Rye, New York"), [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County "Westchester County"), has been open since 1928.

[Legoland New York](/wiki/Legoland_New_York "Legoland New York"), in [Goshen](/wiki/Goshen,_New_York "Goshen, New York"), [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York"), opened in 2021.

Plans were unveiled by New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg on September 27, 2012, for the [New York Wheel](/wiki/New_York_Wheel "New York Wheel"), a giant [Ferris wheel](/wiki/Ferris_wheel "Ferris wheel"), to be built at the northern shore of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), overlooking the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"), [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), and the [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") skyline.[[280]](#cite_note-283)

## Area codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_metropolitan_area&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Area codes")]

The area is served by at least 26 area codes:

* [212](/wiki/Area_code_212 "Area code 212"): Serves [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and is [overlaid](/wiki/Overlay_plan "Overlay plan") with [646](/wiki/Area_code_646 "Area code 646"), [917](/wiki/Area_code_917 "Area code 917"), and [332](/wiki/Area_code_332 "Area code 332").
* [718](/wiki/Area_code_718 "Area code 718"): Serves all other boroughs of New York City and is overlaid with [347](/wiki/Area_code_347 "Area code 347"), [917](/wiki/Area_code_917 "Area code 917"), and [929](/wiki/Area_code_929 "Area code 929").
* [917](/wiki/Area_code_917 "Area code 917"): Serves all of New York City.
* [516 & 363](/wiki/Area_codes_516_and_363 "Area codes 516 and 363"): Serve [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York").
* [631 & 934](/wiki/Area_codes_631_and_934 "Area codes 631 and 934"): Serve [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York").
* [914](/wiki/Area_code_914 "Area code 914"): Serves [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County "Westchester County").
* [845](/wiki/Area_code_845 "Area code 845"): Serves the [Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley") counties of southern New York State.
* [570 & 272](/wiki/Area_code_570 "Area code 570"): Serves Pike County in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").
* [203 & 475](/wiki/Area_code_203_and_475 "Area code 203 and 475"): Serves southwestern [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut").
* [860 & 959](/wiki/Area_code_860_and_959 "Area code 860 and 959"): Serves the rest of Connecticut not served by 203 or 475.
* [201](/wiki/Area_codes_201_and_551 "Area codes 201 and 551"): Serves most of Bergen County, as well as parts of Essex, Hudson, and Passaic in [Northern](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey") [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), and is overlaid with [551](/wiki/Area_code_551 "Area code 551").
* [973](/wiki/Area_code_973 "Area code 973"): Serves portions of Bergen, Essex, Hudson, Morris, Passaic, Sussex, and portions of Union County in Northern New Jersey, and is overlaid with [862](/wiki/Area_code_862 "Area code 862").
* [908](/wiki/Area_code_908 "Area code 908"): Serves communities in Union County, Somerset County, northern parts of Middlesex County, Hunterdon County, Warren County, and Morris County as well as some cell phones in Monmouth County in [Central](/wiki/Central_Jersey "Central Jersey") New Jersey.
* [732](/wiki/Area_code_732 "Area code 732"): Serves Middlesex County, Somerset County, portions of Union County, Monmouth and northern Ocean counties in Central New Jersey; overlaid with [848](/wiki/Area_code_848 "Area code 848").
* [609 & 640](/wiki/Area_codes_609_and_640 "Area codes 609 and 640"): Serves Mercer County and parts of Middlesex, Monmouth, and Ocean Counties in Central New Jersey.
